dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 1 responding for reported breathing problems at 21st and Broad.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What's your name and what made you call 911?
Yeah, I'm Christopher. I've got a hard time breathing. It's been getting worse tonight.
Mr. Christopher, can you tell me your name again and where you are?
Your eyes are open naturally without prompting. That's good.
Can you tell me today's date for me, please?
If I tap your toe, feel free to wiggle your foot for me.
Mr. Christopher, I need to assess your airway. Is it open and free?
Your chest seems to rise appropriately-let me count your respiratory effort now.
Your pulse is strong and regular. I don't notice any signs of pallor.
Mr. Christopher, your breaths appear shallow. How do they feel to you?
When did your shortness of breath first show up? Was it sudden or slow?
It started this evening. I've had it for a few days actually. I missed dialysis four days ago.
Have you used any inhalers or medications to ease it?
I took my inhaler but it's not helping much.
Are there any allergies or medications we should be aware of?
I'm allergic to Tylenol. Otherwise, I take no medications regularly.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate your breathing difficulty now?
Maybe a 7. It's not sharp but feels tighter with each breath.
Did anything help ease it or make it worse today?
No relief from my inhaler. The swelling in my legs got worse today.
Mr. Christopher, let me check for swelling on your legs.
There's significant swelling in both lower limbs. That seems new or getting worse.
Your eyes appear normal. Can you remember who I am?
I'm going to listen to your lungs now. Please take deep breaths.
Mr. Christopher, I'll count your pulse. Just take it slow and steady.
Your breathing is fast but not labored. I'll count at 22 per minute.
We're going to use a cuff to check your blood pressure now. I'll place it on your arm.
I'll use a finger clip to check your oxygen saturation. Just slide your finger in, okay?
Your breath and alertness are normal. We're not measuring glucose.
We need to check your heart rhythm. Okay if we do an ECG?
Mr. Christopher, I'm going to give you oxygen at 2 liters to assist with your breathing. Is that okay?
Based on your symptoms and history, we believe it's a COPD or asthma exacerbation. We'll follow the respiratory distress protocol.
Let's check your respiratory rate again. Breathe as you normally do.
Your oxygen level has improved slightly. You notice the difference too?
Any new symptoms or worsening since we started oxygen?
Your breathing sounds clear everywhere. You're not wheezing but it is mildly labored.
You're not diaphoretic, and your neck veins look normal.
Mr. Christopher, we need to transfer you to the stretcher. Will you let me stand and pivot you quietly?
Your stretcher is ready and secured. You comfortable with this?
Just a quick recheck of your pulse-stable at 93 beats per minute.
Rechecking your breathing rate at 21. Still fast, but slower than before.
Blood pressure is down now at 160/90-still stable but slightly improved.
EKG shows a first-degree AV block, possible left atrial enlargement, and some supraventricular tachycardia.
Your oxygen saturation is 94% initially, which we've now improved to 97%. That's a good response to treatment.
Mr. Christopher, you mentioned swelling in your legs and missed dialysis. Have you felt any cramping or weakness?
You also said you had a metallic taste sometimes. Have you had any other unusual symptoms that might go along with that?
You may be experiencing some hyperkalemia symptoms due to missing dialysis. That can increase shortness of breath and swelling, so we'll keep monitoring that.
You mentioned a metallic taste earlier. Have you noticed that more?
Your dialysis is important. How have you felt without it?
You said you wanted to go to St. Mary's-no issues with that.
Your symptoms and vitals are stable, so St. Mary's remains the best choice.
Your oxygen saturation shows 97%. That's a positive sign.
Since this is a COPD or possible asthma flare, muscle strength wasn't assessed.
You're now secure on the stretcher. We'll support you during transport.
One last set of vitals before we start the transport process. Everything's stable.
All findings documented for the hospital. Includes your GCS of 15, BP 160/90, and first-degree AV block.
We're beginning transport now. I'll remain with you throughout the ride.
No critical changes-staying stable and continuing transport to the hospital.
Final O2 reading before arrival was 97%. Recorded successfully.
You're in good hands. Let's go.
Any change in symptoms or worsening since last assessment? Still no chest pain or vomiting?
Your work of breathing remains stable and no new issues. We're all set for transport.
St. Mary's as per your request, and we're moving forward.
Your initial O2 was 94%, but it improved with treatment. We've documented everything from your history and vitals.
You did not have signs of croup. We watched for it, but nothing of note.
The suspect of hyperkalemia is a secondary concern but important for the ED to address given your renal condition.
No critical changes-staying stable and continuing transport to the hospital.
All findings and interventions have been processed for the hospital.
We're beginning transport now. I'll remain with you throughout the ride.
